item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sent a email, hoping I can use my PayPal cash for this.

item.title: Re: Timeline
item.content:encoded:
Never mind my comment, I think I get it now. Tony Blair’s political career is just an abstract concept so on sale…

I think this article is way underrated. It’s original and it has something to say and at the same time really cool. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is great.

The only part I don't like is… an intergalactic extraterrestrial corporation purchasing ownership of the concept of SCP-2557 definitely counts as anomalous, so it certainly deserves to be in the database as an SCP. But it's not. Like, right now there's a barber paradox deal going on, but the paradox would be solved by creating an actual SCP entry, with a number other than 2557. That SCP entry's description ought to be: "SCP-XXXX is the concept of SCP Foundation's database listing for SCP-2557".

Like, I can't imagine the Foundation would just allow this specific page to be anomalous without attempting to document the anomaly somewhere far away from the anomalous database entry. That's how it works with, say, SCP-033, the missing number. The number itself is the anomalous concept. Can you imagine if SCP-033's database number were the missing number itself? SCP-Θ? I know there's a great antimemetics tale about that idea, but it specifically has SCP-Θ be something other than the concept of Θ itself

Lile, idk, it's sorta hard to get across what I'm saying, i don't quite know how to describe my objection

Maybe the answer is just that if the Foundation had a page documenting the anomaly "SCP-2557", it would get charged with conversion of property, or maybe part of the negotiations between O5 and the corporation included that the Foundation would not use the concept of SCP-2557, including the idea of "containing" SCP-2557. Err, SCP-2557-as-an-anomaly, not SCP-2557-as-a-database-entry. I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel mildly uncomfortable now that the author has my address via the email thing… I didn't think it actually worked. I put in jokes for the other boxes but the address was accidental…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone in corporate divisions that are usually free of this kind of horsehockey, the corpratese spam, corporate trademark invasions, and testimonials are spot on. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good example of a gimmick that's enough to carry an entire article. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love how unique it is, but I can’t get the form to send.

item.title: Is there a picture
item.content:encoded:
for us folks who still want to read a version of the email?

Cause when I filled out the form ( with the comment: "what happened to site 13?" XD ), it sent an error message saying: "The form data could not be sent to the specified email address."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sends an email to Kate Mctriss, but I suspect she isn't maintaining this project any more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does the form do? It didn't seem to do anything for me. Did I choose the wrong universe or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't get my email…I got a message saying I already had an account……..but I don't remember and there is no way to reset….damn this feels exactly like dealing with an online company.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope not, because I would also like to buy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it too late for me to invest in the concept of number of upvotes to SCP-2557? Because I think I would have made a killing if I started earlier back there.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes hello I would like to sell my gender dysphoria.

+1, loved the format screw.

item.title: I sent my form
item.content:encoded: I actually sent a form with extremely fake information, and I dont know what am I gonna get?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, I was hoping for an email too. Oh well.

item.title: 2 words
item.content:encoded: Free. Use.

item.title: Re: Timeline
item.content:encoded:
or investing in political careers never was illegal.

the sentence states that envelope is the leader since 1997. not they are operating since 1997.

item.title: Re: Timeline
item.content:encoded: She went to a country/planet/universe where it was already legal.

item.title: Timeline
item.content:encoded:
Sorry just one thing that I noticed:
The deregulation and privatisation of abstractions happened during the 1997 "Divinetic Althing" to my understanding?

Then how Dana G. "invested in the abstract concept of Tony Blair's political career in 1984"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out standing SCP. Loving the format and just everything about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP to death. What at first seemed like an "infohazard gone haywire" format screw turned out to be much more. Love the creativity in this one!

Oh, by the way: should inspiration strike me, would it be alright if I wrote a tale based around this? I feel like I've got an inkling of an idea, though I don't know when I'd be able to give it substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone should sue. I invested in Number of tetanus cases in Santa Clara Kaiser Hospital, CA and nothing even changed. Although, I guess that's on me for trusting something implied but not actually explicitly stated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a commercialized wishing well

reality warping as a service

where can I purchase a gift card?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something I missed on my first reading: the first testimonial implies that you can not only invest in abstract concepts, but change reality through said investment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the most dangerous skips I've ever seen and there's no way to contain it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. I wonder how many spam mails does he/she receives every day?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A great example of a gimmicky SCP that actually has something to it besides the actual gimmick. +1

P.S. I thought filling out the form at the end would send an e-mail to the person filling out the form. While I was thinking that I was just doing something wrong, I think I might've just sent a little spam to the author… oops lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote it, but the concept of upvotes upvotes are now a holding of Envelope Logistics®

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ummm, someone can help me modifying the first picture? I need it for the Spanish branch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sends the information to the author of the article. Not sure if via wikidot pm or email, but yeah. Not sure if Kate she pays any attention to wherever it goes, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Er… What happens when I fulfill the form and click "Send"? Just curious…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was an interesting exception to that rule! I read the whole thing, knew there was nothing behind the curtain, and still upvoted for the curtain.

item.title: The form
item.content:encoded: Do you really update the SCP, if you find something interesting in your inbox?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There should be a tale featuring this and SCP-004-J…that would be hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the presentation and formatting. Quite funny. Everything else has already been said

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best format screw I've seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me very uncomfortable. I love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the fact that this SCP is interactable, this is definitely going in my top ten favorite SCP, great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Thou hast multiplied thy merchants above the stars of heaven: the cankerworm spoileth, and flieth away." - Nahum 3:16 (KJV)

As a side note, I think including this actual verse in the SCP article itself would be a nice idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the base idea, even though it comes off as a bit "lolrandom gimmicky" at times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oops

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first of these links redirects to the Flickr homepage. Is there an updated link for the first picture's attribution?

It worked the second time I tried, nvm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a story; the Foundation got hoodwinked/tricked/forced to sell this slot in their database to the company, and now a reality-bending and uncontained company has found themselves a nice home in the Foundation's database, able to manipulate employees into putting stock into concepts if they so choose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ambituous format screw, complete lack of story or substance.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is gimmicky to the point that it just really irritates me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What will happen if I fill out that thing? Will someone hijack my computer or somethin' or steal my email? :C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THE INVESTMENT METHODS.

"Blood rites. Lifespan partitioning. Gift card."

Brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really unique and I love it, their is nothing I need to say here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The people who invested in the troll face before it got popular are probably as happy as those who invested in Apple a while back

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only thing left to do is find the proper jurisdiction in which to sue for breach of contract…

(Sorry, couldn't help it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And what if the reason it's keter…

Was to attract more attention to itself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what if the only way that they could contain this was by giving it ad space and letting researchers deduct what it was from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Հայոց այբուբեն Hayots aybuben :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couldn't the Foundation attempt to contact whatever government has legal authority to dismiss Envelope's contracts based on the idea that the seller had no legal right to sell the SCP, thereby making Envelope's…

…

Dammit. The Foundation sold Envelope an empty SCP slot as adspace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Whatever SCP-2557 was before (whether Envelope itself or something else), the Foundation can't contain it without permission from Envelope because Envelope owns the idea of containing it. They can't even add angry addenda to the article about the (il)legal aspects of Envelope's ownership of the concept, because they can't change the article because it relates to the concept of containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's what you get for investing in other people's suffering/pain/discomfort/[insert synonym here]

I say sell your stock and buy some in the concept of reduced dysphoria — then we all win :)

(I'd so do that, and then use the dividends to pay for surgery and then it'd pay off more and… Yep, you just got my upvote)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'll probably get some sound upvoting advise and end up with a solid portfolio. But you may be the target of an insider trading investigation by The Foundation. I wouldn't risk it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cancer rates of Selkirk, Manitoba. I lost my shit right there and then. +1 Love to see stuff close to home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the most bizarre thing I have ever read on this site.
